title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff after deployment
symptoms: Pod status shows CrashLoopBackOff in kubectl get pods
Restart counter keeps incrementing every few seconds
Application endpoints return 502 from the ingress
Readiness probe never succeeds
root_cause: Container process exits with a non-zero status code shortly after start
Typical causes: missing environment variables, bad config map mount path, failing database connection, OOMKilled due to low memory limits
Kubelet backs off exponentially between restarts causing the CrashLoopBackOff state
diagnosis: Inspect pod events: kubectl describe pod <pod>
Stream the previous container logs: kubectl logs <pod> --previous
Check resource usage and OOM signals: kubectl get events --field-selector involvedObject.name=<pod>
Validate the image runs locally: docker run --rm <image>
fix: Fix the underlying error surfaced in `kubectl logs --previous`
Increase memory limits if the pod was OOMKilled:

```yaml
resources:
  requests:
    memory: 256Mi
    cpu: 100m
  limits:
    memory: 512Mi
    cpu: 500m
```
Restart the rollout once fixed: `kubectl rollout restart deployment/<name>`
prevention: Add a liveness and readiness probe that match real application health
Run `kubectl apply --dry-run=server` in CI to catch schema errors
Ship structured logs and wire alerts on restart count > 3 in 5m

title: ImagePullBackOff when rolling out a new container image
symptoms: Pods stay in ImagePullBackOff or ErrImagePull
kubectl describe shows 'unauthorized: authentication required' or 'manifest unknown'
New deployments never become Ready
root_cause: Registry credentials missing or expired in the namespace
Image tag does not exist or was overwritten
Private registry not whitelisted on the nodes
diagnosis: kubectl describe pod <pod> and read the Events section
Verify the image exists: `docker pull <image>` from a worker node
Inspect the imagePullSecret: kubectl get secret regcred -o yaml
fix: Recreate the pull secret:

```bash
kubectl create secret docker-registry regcred \
  --docker-server=registry.example.com \
  --docker-username=$USER \
  --docker-password=$TOKEN \
  --namespace=prod
```
Reference it in the service account or pod spec:

```yaml
imagePullSecrets:
  - name: regcred
```
prevention: Pin images by digest (sha256) instead of mutable tags
Rotate registry tokens via a secret manager with expiry alerts
Mirror critical images to an internal registry

title: Pods stay Pending due to insufficient node resources
symptoms: Pods remain in Pending state for minutes
Events show 0/5 nodes are available: insufficient cpu
HPA cannot scale workload
root_cause: Requests exceed available allocatable resources on all nodes
Taints/tolerations or node selectors block scheduling
Cluster autoscaler disabled or hitting max node count
diagnosis: kubectl describe pod <pod> → Events
kubectl top nodes to view live capacity
kubectl get nodes -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,CPU:.status.allocatable.cpu,MEM:.status.allocatable.memory
fix: Reduce pod requests to a realistic value
Enable or raise the max size of the cluster autoscaler:

```bash
eksctl scale nodegroup --cluster prod --name workers --nodes-max 20
```
Add a tolerations block if nodes are tainted
prevention: Use VPA in recommendation mode to size requests
Set PodDisruptionBudgets before draining nodes
Alert on pending pods > 2 minutes

title: Horizontal Pod Autoscaler continuously scaling up and down
symptoms: Pod count oscillates between minimum and maximum replicas
HPA metrics show frequent scale events
Application experiences brief outages during scale transitions
CPU/memory metrics hovering near threshold boundary
root_cause: HPA thresholds set too low causing sensitivity to normal load variations
Metrics collection delay causing stale data for scaling decisions
Scale-down and stabilization periods too short
Application has bursty traffic patterns
diagnosis: Check HPA events: kubectl describe hpa <hpa-name>
Review metrics: kubectl get hpa <hpa-name> -w
Examine current and desired replica counts
Analyze application metrics to understand load patterns
fix: Adjust HPA thresholds to accommodate normal load variations
Increase stabilization window to prevent rapid scale-down
Implement custom metrics for more accurate scaling decisions
Use metrics with appropriate delay for stable scaling
prevention: Set appropriate thresholds based on load testing results
Configure stabilization windows for scale-down events
Use predictive autoscaling for predictable traffic patterns
Monitor HPA behavior and adjust parameters over time

title: ConfigMap changes not reflected in running pods
symptoms: Updated configuration values not visible in application
Pods continue using old configuration after ConfigMap update
Application restart required to pick up changes
Rolling update of deployment does not refresh config
root_cause: ConfigMap mounted as volume but not configured for automatic reload
Application not watching for file changes in mounted volume
ConfigMap update triggered but pod not restarted
Using environment variables instead of volume mount
diagnosis: Check ConfigMap version: kubectl get configmap <name> -o yaml
Verify mounted volume in pod: kubectl describe pod <pod>
Check file contents in pod: kubectl exec <pod> -- cat /path/to/config
Review application code for config reload logic
fix: Implement application logic to watch and reload config files
Use rolling restart after ConfigMap update: kubectl rollout restart deployment <name>
Consider using tools like Reloader for automatic restarts
For env vars, trigger pod restart on ConfigMap change
prevention: Design applications to hot-reload configuration when possible
Use ConfigMap versioning to track changes
Document configuration update procedures
Consider using custom controllers for config synchronization

title: Service DNS resolution failing intermittently
symptoms: Applications cannot resolve service names to cluster IPs
DNS queries timeout or return NXDOMAIN
Service works with IP address but not DNS name
Issue affects only some namespaces or services
root_cause: CoreDNS pods not running or overloaded
DNS configuration misconfigured in kube-dns config
Network policies blocking DNS traffic
Service not created or not in same namespace
diagnosis: Check CoreDNS pods: kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns
Test DNS from pod: kubectl exec <pod> -- nslookup kubernetes.default
Review CoreDNS logs: kubectl logs -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns
Verify service exists: kubectl get svc <service-name>
fix: Restart CoreDNS pods: kubectl rollout restart deployment coredns -n kube-system
Scale CoreDNS deployment if overloaded
Check and fix network policies blocking DNS traffic
Verify service is in correct namespace or use FQDN
prevention: Monitor CoreDNS resource usage and scale as needed
Use NodeLocal DNSCache to reduce DNS latency
Set up alerts for DNS resolution failures
Test DNS configuration after cluster changes

title: Pod disruptions causing downtime despite PodDisruptionBudget
symptoms: Node maintenance causes service disruption
Pods evicted despite PDB configured
Availability drops below minimum required
Cluster autoscaler terminates pods unexpectedly
root_cause: PDB not correctly configured for workload
Min available set too high for current replica count
PDB not applied to all necessary pods
Node drain not respecting PDB constraints
diagnosis: Check PDB status: kubectl get pdb
Describe PDB: kubectl describe pdb <pdb-name>
Review current vs desired pod counts
Check events for PDB violations
fix: Configure PDB with appropriate min available or max unavailable
Ensure replica count satisfies PDB requirements
Apply PDB to all deployments and statefulsets
Use PDB with percentage-based limits for flexibility
prevention: Always create PDBs for critical workloads
Test PDB behavior during maintenance windows
Monitor PDB status and violations
Document PDB requirements for each service

title: Ingress controller returning 502 errors for backend services
symptoms: Ingress returns 502 Bad Gateway for valid routes
Service endpoints show as not ready
Backend pods healthy but ingress cannot reach them
Issue occurs intermittently under load
root_cause: Service selector not matching pod labels
Backend service not exposing correct port
Ingress controller health check failing
Network policies blocking ingress to backend pods
diagnosis: Check service endpoints: kubectl get endpoints <service-name>
Verify pod labels match service selector
Review ingress controller logs
Test network policies: kubectl get networkpolicies
fix: Fix service selector to match pod labels
Ensure service port matches container port
Configure proper health checks in ingress annotation
Update network policies to allow ingress traffic
prevention: Use label selectors consistently across services and pods
Implement proper health checks for all services
Test ingress configuration in staging environment
Monitor ingress error rates and set up alerts

title: etcd database growing too large causing performance issues
symptoms: Kubernetes API server responses slow
etcd database size exceeding available disk space
Cluster operations timing out
etcd logs showing warning about database size
root_cause: etcd not configured for automatic compaction
Too many historical events retained
Frequent resource changes creating event churn
etcd quota not properly sized for cluster workload
diagnosis: Check etcd database size: ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl endpoint status --write-out=table
Review etcd compaction status
Monitor disk usage on etcd nodes
Check Kubernetes event retention settings
fix: Enable etcd auto-compaction in API server configuration
Manually compact etcd history: ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl compact <revision>
Defragment etcd database: ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl defrag
Reduce event retention period in cluster configuration
prevention: Enable automatic etcd compaction from cluster start
Set appropriate event retention periods
Monitor etcd database size and disk usage
Plan etcd capacity based on cluster size and workload

title: Permission denied errors when accessing Kubernetes resources
symptoms: Users or services receive 'User cannot <verb> <resource>' errors
Applications fail to create or modify resources
kubectl commands fail with authorization errors
ServiceAccount cannot access required API endpoints
root_cause: RBAC Role or ClusterRole not bound to user or service account
Role missing required verbs for the operation
Resource name or namespace not matching in role definition
Cluster-wide operation attempted with namespaced role
diagnosis: Check current permissions: kubectl auth can-i <verb> <resource>
Review role bindings: kubectl get rolebindings,clusterrolebindings
Inspect role definitions: kubectl get role,clusterrole -o yaml
Check service account permissions: kubectl get sa
fix: Create or update Role/ClusterRole with required permissions
Bind role to user or service account with RoleBinding or ClusterRoleBinding
Use kubectl create rolebinding to create missing bindings
For service accounts, ensure automountServiceAccountToken is enabled
prevention: Follow principle of least privilege when defining roles
Document required permissions for each application
Use RBAC audit tools to review permissions regularly
Test RBAC configuration in non-production environment

title: Pods restarted unnecessarily due to failing liveness probe
symptoms: Pods restarting repeatedly despite application being healthy
Liveness probe failing while readiness probe succeeds
Application logs show no errors but pod restarts
CrashLoopBackOff caused by liveness probe failures
root_cause: Liveness probe checking wrong endpoint or port
Probe timeout too short for application response
Application temporarily unresponsive during startup or load
Liveness and readiness probes using same endpoint incorrectly
diagnosis: Check probe status: kubectl describe pod <pod>
Test probe endpoint manually from within pod
Review probe configuration in pod spec
Check application logs during probe failures
fix: Fix liveness probe endpoint to check actual application health
Increase initialDelaySeconds to allow application startup
Increase timeoutSeconds and periodSeconds for slower applications
Consider removing liveness probe if application can self-recover
prevention: Design liveness probes to check for conditions requiring restart
Use separate endpoints for liveness and readiness probes
Test probe configurations in staging environment
Monitor restart reasons to identify probe issues
